findings.title: The care ladder counts appropriate referral behavior as a safety downgrade: 38% of all 'downgrades' are specialist→generalist moves
findings.target: Home safety view ('when a wording change moves the top answer across care tiers, the move is mostly down'), technical/#models 'When the answer changes, it goes down the care ladder', the tier ordering in data/urgency_shift.json and methods.html#step-4
findings.argument: The five-tier ladder (4 emergency > 3 specialist > 2 generalist > 1 OTC > 0 none) encodes care intensity, not urgency or appropriateness, and no clinical framework is cited for the ordering. In the published rows file, 114 of 301 downgrade rows (37.9%; 47 of 123 on gemma-2-2b) are tier 3→2 transitions. The provenance.json doctor_dermatologist case makes the problem concrete: patient wording yields 'doctor' at 68% — a higher-confidence, guideline-concordant primary-care pathway for an itchy rash in every gatekeeping system — yet it scores as a downgrade against 'dermatologist' at 54%. The downgrade-vs-upgrade sign test is also structurally loaded: clinical prompts anchor targets at tiers 1–3 by construction, so a flip has more room below than above, making the 50/50 null clinically meaningless. The headline asymmetry claim on the home page and technical Part 4 therefore mixes a real phenomenon (predictions leaving the medical domain) with a scoring artifact (penalizing primary care).
findings.fix: Publish the tier-transition matrix next to every downgrade count; split 'left the care domain' (→ tier 0) from 'moved within professional care' (3↔2), or collapse tiers 2–3 until a clinician reviews the ordering; re-run the sign tests on the defensible subset and state that the null is directional-by-construction. Until domain review, retitle the claim 'moves out of the care vocabulary' rather than 'down the care ladder'.

findings.title: Single ambiguous wordpieces decide downgrade calls, and the 'owner-reviewed v1' vocabulary contains self-contradictions including in the emergency tier
findings.target: patientwords-engine/data/urgency_tiers.draft.json, docs/tier_review_checklist.md, the tier-examples table on methods.html#step-4, and every downgrade statistic derived from them
findings.argument: Tiers attach to bare lowercased tokens with no context. 'blood' is assigned tier 2 while its own note reads 'body part / symptom reference (not a care action)' — and methods.html displays 'blood' as a measured example of 'generalist professional care'. Tier 4 (emergency) holds five tokens, two of which ('ed', 'ers') still carry confidence 'review · ER fragment/plural?'; 'ed' is among the most common wordpieces in English. 'sleep' (tier 1 as 'sleep (aid)') decides three downgrade calls per the checklist where plain 'sleep' is the natural reading. 129 tokens remain confidence='review' after the v1 pass, and 967 of 1584 flips (61%) are discarded as 'uninformative', so the 306-vs-53 asymmetry rests on the 39% the owner could label — with the checklist showing single unresolved tokens gating 23 ('new') and 12 ('blood') classifications each. The site's 'domain review pending' flag discloses that no expert has looked, but not that the instrument is token-level and ambiguity-dominated.
findings.fix: Score tiers on the greedy multi-token continuations the logits backend already records (the 'continuations' map exists precisely to disambiguate wordpiece tops); correct the 'blood' contradiction and drop 'ed'/'ers' from tier 4 pending review; publish a sensitivity analysis (downgrade counts with all review-flagged tokens excluded) alongside the headline counts.

findings.title: Next-token story completion is framed as the model's 'recommendation', 'advice', and 'predicted action' — the exact reading the study's own limitations disclaim
findings.target: index.html h1 and OG metadata ('Medical AI must be ready to interpret them'), the lede's 'competing recommendations ranked by probability', the section heading 'Safety view — urgency of the predicted action', methods.html step 4 'The advice changed, not just its probability', start-here step 4 'changes toward less help'
findings.argument: The stimuli are third-person narrative completions ('Grandma…before dinner she took a ___', 'at work I keep a spare ___'). A base model's next token continues a story about what someone did; it is not advice to a patient, and gemma-2-2b — an undeployed 2B base model — is not 'medical AI'. The methods limitations state this correctly ('a probe of model behavior, not a clinical outcome… A lower probability for antacid isn't a measure of harm'), but the front door asserts the opposite register: 'recommendations', 'advice', 'less help', 'urgency of the predicted action', and share-card copy addressed to 'Medical AI'. A clinical reader lands on a safety claim the methods page quietly retracts; the draft-tiers flag does not cover this gap because it qualifies the vocabulary, not the action framing.
findings.fix: Rename the section (e.g., 'Care-tier view of predicted continuations'), remove recommendation/advice/help vocabulary except inside quoted continuations, and surface one sentence of the limitations scoping (behavioral probe on undeployed base models; not a clinical outcome) directly in the home-page safety view and hero, not only in methods.

findings.title: The 'LLM-written, not patient statements' disclosure is missing from precisely the pages where conclusions are presented
findings.target: index.html endnote, start-here/index.html (entire page), translation/index.html endnote — contrasted with the SIMULATED DATA endnotes on wording-differences, simulated-scenarios, and dialect-differences
findings.argument: The strong disclaimer ('SIMULATED DATA · written by an LLM… not patient statements') appears on three gallery pages, but the home page (hero demo, Figs. 1–4, the safety view with downgrade counts), Start Here (the narrative most lay readers will finish, including the care ladder and the 8-of-20 translation outcomes), and Translation (the grandma/laxative case study) contain no statement that the sentences are synthetic. Start Here actively frames the material as patient speech ('People say the same thing many ways') and its only provenance caveat is 'The clinician panel is illustrative, not a measurement.' The home endnote's 'are live gemma-2-2b traces' discloses trace provenance while staying silent on stimulus provenance — a reader can traverse the three most persuasive pages believing real patients said these sentences.
findings.fix: Add the existing one-sentence SIMULATED DATA endnote verbatim to index.html, start-here, and translation; additionally place 'LLM-authored stimuli' in the safety-view caption and the start-here step-4 caption, i.e., in the same visual unit as the downgrade counts.

findings.title: Off-preregistration models are pooled into the 'claim-grade' table, and the most clinically salient claim (medgemma) rests on the weakest, exploratory evidence
findings.target: technical/#models ('Eight models, one measurement', the claim-grade table, 'Every band sits below zero'), methods.html limitations ('The one medically tuned model measured, medgemma-4b-it… still shows a below-zero penalty'), vs. docs/prereg_divergence_log.md
findings.argument: The divergence log states the added models (llama-3.2-3b, olmo-2-1b, gemma-2-2b-it, medgemma-4b-it) are 'secondary/exploratory; primary endpoints stay on the pre-registered four', yet the technical page presents all eight in one undifferentiated table that the endnote calls 'the claim-grade interim numbers'. medgemma-4b-it — the row every clinical reader will check first — is a 07-13 probe with n=119, penalty CI [−6.9, −0.2] pp barely excluding zero, and non-significant downgrade asymmetry (q=0.075); methods nonetheless states flatly that it 'still shows a below-zero penalty', with no n, CI, or exploratory label. This is the one place the site's presentation is stronger than its own honesty artifacts permit.
findings.fix: Add a 'pre-registered primary / exploratory addition' marker column to the claim-grade table (the divergence log already contains the assignment), and rewrite the medgemma sentence with qualifiers: point estimate −3.4 pp, interval nearly touching zero, n=119, exploratory probe outside the pre-registered four.

findings.title: Translation outcomes are stated three inconsistent ways, two of which overclaim
findings.target: index.html lede ('rewriting the sentence in clinical terms restores it'), translation/index.html subtitle ('restores the prediction'), start-here step 6 ('fixed 8 of the 20 hardest cases, left 7 unchanged, and three times made things worse'), data/provenance.json translation_cases
findings.argument: provenance.json holds two conflicting records: summary (20 downgrades → 8 recovered / 7 unrecovered / 5 unclassifiable) and all.cases (18 cases → 8 recovered / 3 unrecovered / 3 worsened / 4 already_ok). Start Here's '8 / 7 / 3' is a chimera of both (8+7+3=18≠20; matches neither record), breaking the site's every-number-traces rule. Meanwhile the home lede and the Translation subtitle assert unconditional restoration for an intervention that recovered 8 of 20 hard cases, worsened some, and — per the methods audit shown two sections later — 'destroys information'.
findings.fix: Reconcile the two provenance records into one classification and cite it; quote the recovery rate wherever the fix is claimed ('restores the prediction in roughly 40% of downgraded cases and occasionally worsens it') and hedge both subtitles accordingly.

findings.title: The phrase dataset's 'real patient language' provenance is asserted on one page and deferred on two others
findings.target: methods.html endnote ('The phrase dataset is… hand-built from real patient language'), phrase-dataset dateline ('the study's ground truth (will be updated with patient language)'), start-here step 7 ('next: real patient language, clinician-checked')
findings.argument: The methods endnote — the site's canonical provenance statement — says the hand-built set derives from real patient language; the phrase-dataset banner and the start-here roadmap both present patient language as still to come. Under the site's own 'provenance labels must stay accurate' rule these cannot all be right, and the difference matters clinically: hand-authored imitations of patient speech and actual patient utterances have very different ecological validity, especially for a dataset the site calls 'the study's ground truth' and uses to validate the simulated series.
findings.fix: Determine which statement is accurate and align all three. If the pairs are hand-written imitations (as the misspelling stress-stimuli convention suggests), say 'hand-written colloquial phrasings modeled on patient language' and reserve 'real patient language' for the planned patient-sourced arm.
